infobox.key: [Papyrus 46](/wiki/Papyrus_46 "Papyrus 46"), one of the oldest [New Testament papyri](/wiki/New_Testament_papyri "New Testament papyri"), showing [2 Corinthians 11:33–12:9](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=2+Corinthians+11:33–12:9&version=nrsvue)
infobox.value: | Gospels and [Acts](/wiki/Acts_of_the_Apostles_(genre) "Acts of the Apostles (genre)")  [Four Evangelists](/wiki/Four_Evangelists "Four Evangelists")   * + [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew")   + [Mark](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mark "Gospel of Mark")   + [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke")   + [John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John")  ---   [Lukan Acts](/wiki/Luke%E2%80%93Acts "Luke–Acts")   * [Acts of the Apostles](/wiki/Acts_of_the_Apostles "Acts of the Apostles")

originally meant the [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") message ("[the gospel](/wiki/The_gospel "The gospel")"), but in the second century [AD](/wiki/Anno_domino "Anno domino") the term *euangélion* ([Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek_language "Koine Greek language"): εὐαγγέλιον, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'good news', from which the English word originated as a [calque](/wiki/Calque "Calque")) came to be used also for the books in which the message was reported.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrossLivingstone2005697-1) In this sense a gospel can be defined as a loose-knit, episodic narrative of the words and deeds of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity"), culminating in [his trial](/wiki/Trial_of_Jesus "Trial of Jesus") and [death](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), and concluding with various reports of his [post-resurrection appearances](/wiki/Post-resurrection_appearances_of_Jesus "Post-resurrection appearances of Jesus").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlexander200616-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Papyrus_BnF_Suppl._gr._1120_ii_3_%28Gregory-Aland_papyrus_P4%29_-_Gospel_of_Matthew%27s_title%2C_euangelion_kata_Maththaion.jpg/250px-Papyrus_BnF_Suppl._gr._1120_ii_3_%28Gregory-Aland_papyrus_P4%29_-_Gospel_of_Matthew%27s_title%2C_euangelion_kata_Maththaion.jpg)](/wiki/File:Papyrus_BnF_Suppl._gr._1120_ii_3_(Gregory-Aland_papyrus_P4)_-_Gospel_of_Matthew%27s_title,_euangelion_kata_Maththaion.jpg)

Fragment of a flyleaf with the title of the [Gospel of Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew"), ευαγγελιον κ̣ατ̣α μαθ᾽θαιον (*Euangelion kata Maththaion*). From [Papyrus 4](/wiki/Papyrus_4 "Papyrus 4") (c. AD 200), it is the earliest manuscript title for Matthew and one of the earliest manuscript titles for any gospel.

**Gospel** originally meant the [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") message ("[the gospel](/wiki/The_gospel "The gospel")"), but in the second century [AD](/wiki/Anno_domino "Anno domino") the term *euangélion* ([Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek_language "Koine Greek language"): εὐαγγέλιον, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'good news', from which the English word originated as a [calque](/wiki/Calque "Calque")) came to be used also for the books in which the message was reported.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrossLivingstone2005697-1) In this sense a gospel can be defined as a loose-knit, episodic narrative of the words and deeds of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity"), culminating in [his trial](/wiki/Trial_of_Jesus "Trial of Jesus") and [death](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), and concluding with various reports of his [post-resurrection appearances](/wiki/Post-resurrection_appearances_of_Jesus "Post-resurrection appearances of Jesus").[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAlexander200616-2)

The Gospels are widely considered to represent a variation of Greco-Roman biography.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELincoln2004133-4) They are meant to convince people that Jesus was a charismatic miracle-working holy man, providing examples for readers to emulate.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhrman199952-5)[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunn2005174-6)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermes201332-7) As such, they present the Christian message of the second half of the first century AD.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithLe_Donne2012[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_July_2021]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (July_2021)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-8) Modern [biblical scholars](/wiki/Biblical_studies "Biblical studies") are therefore cautious of relying on the gospels uncritically as historical documents, and although they afford a good idea of Jesus' public career, critical study has largely failed to distinguish his original ideas from those of the later [Christian authors](/wiki/Early_Christian_writers "Early Christian writers"),[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeith2016-9)[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithLe_Donne2012-10) and the focus of research has therefore shifted to Jesus as remembered by his followers,[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunn1995371–372-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunn2003-12)[[note 1]](#cite_note-Dunn_1995_teachings_as_remembered-13)[[note 2]](#cite_note-Dunn_2003_back_to_Jesus-14) and understanding the Gospels themselves.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeith2012-15)

The [canonical](/wiki/Biblical_canon "Biblical canon") gospels are the four which appear in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"). They were written between AD 66 and 110, which puts their composition likely within the lifetimes of various eyewitnesses.[[14]](#cite_note-van_Os_2011_57,_83-16)[[15]](#cite_note-Sanders_1996_5-17)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerkins1998241-18)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReddish2011108,_144-19)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELincoln200518-20) The texts are anonymous and generally not viewed as eyewitness accounts, though this may be partly the result of dubious [form-critical](/wiki/Form_criticism "Form criticism") assumptions.[[19]](#cite_note-:4-21) The gospels are products of literarily creative authors (which did involve claiming consulting eyewitnesses).[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReddish201113,_42-22)[[21]](#cite_note-Byrskog_2000_18–28,_69-23)[[22]](#cite_note-Rodriguez_57-24)[[23]](#cite_note-Becker_2017_39–40,_59-25)[[24]](#cite_note-:3-26) [Mark](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mark "Gospel of Mark") was the first to be written,[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodacre200156-27)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoring200613–14-28) followed by [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke"), which in the [two-source hypothesis](/wiki/Two-source_hypothesis "Two-source hypothesis") independently used Mark and a hypothetical source [Q](/wiki/Q_source "Q source"), though alternative hypotheses that posit the direct use of Matthew by Luke or vice versa without Q are rising.[[27]](#cite_note-:02-29)[[28]](#cite_note-TSP20222-30)[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevine20096-31) Scholars differ on the reliability of the traditions found in the gospels.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunn1995-32)[[31]](#cite_note-Wright_1998-33)[[32]](#cite_note-Bockmuehl_2006_166–178-34)[[33]](#cite_note-McIver_2011-35)[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhrman1997-36)[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEValantasisBleyleHaugh2009-37) The author of [John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John") may have been aware of the [synoptic gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_gospels "Synoptic gospels") (Matthew, Mark and Luke), and the gospel's historical context remains debated.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBynum20127,_12-38)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurge2014309-39) The synoptic gospels are the primary sources for reconstructing Christ's [ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus"), while John is used less since it differs from the synoptics.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanders2010-40)[[note 3]](#cite_note-Sanders_primary_sources-41) According to the manuscript evidence and citation frequency by the early [Church Fathers](/wiki/Church_Fathers "Church Fathers"), Matthew and John were the most popular gospels while Luke and Mark were less popular in the early centuries of the Church.[[39]](#cite_note-42)

Many [non-canonical gospels](/wiki/New_Testament_apocrypha "New Testament apocrypha") were also written after the four canonical gospels, and like them advocate for the theological views of their authors.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPetersen201051-43)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECulpepper199966-44) Important examples include the gospels of [Thomas](/wiki/Gospel_of_Thomas "Gospel of Thomas"), [Peter](/wiki/Gospel_of_Peter "Gospel of Peter"), [Judas](/wiki/Gospel_of_Judas "Gospel of Judas"), and [Mary](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mary "Gospel of Mary"); [infancy gospels](/wiki/Infancy_gospels "Infancy gospels") such as that of [James](/wiki/Gospel_of_James "Gospel of James") (the first to introduce the [perpetual virginity of Mary](/wiki/Perpetual_virginity_of_Mary "Perpetual virginity of Mary")); and [gospel harmonies](/wiki/Gospel_harmony "Gospel harmony") such as the [Diatessaron](/wiki/Diatessaron "Diatessaron").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gospel&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

*Gospel* is the [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") translation of the [Hellenistic Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek") term εὐαγγέλιον (evangélion), meaning "good news";[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWoodhead20044-45) this may be seen from analysis of ευαγγέλιον (evangélion) (εὖ (ef) "good" + ἄγγελος (ángelos) "messenger" + -ιον (ion) [diminutive](/wiki/Diminutive "Diminutive") suffix). The Greek term was [Latinized](/wiki/Latinisation_(literature) "Latinisation (literature)") as *evangelium* in the [Vulgate](/wiki/Vulgate "Vulgate"), and translated into [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") as *bona annuntiatio*. In Old English, it was translated as *gōdspel* (*gōd* "good" + *spel* "news"). The Old English term was retained as *gospel* in [Middle English Bible translations](/wiki/Middle_English_Bible_translations "Middle English Bible translations") and hence remains in use also in [Modern English](/wiki/Modern_English "Modern English").

[Encyclopaedia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica") defines gospel as stemming from gōdspel "good story/news," a calque translating Latin evangelium from Greek εὐαγγέλιον "good news."[[43]](#cite_note-46)

### Contents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gospel&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Contents")]

The four [canonical](/wiki/Biblical_canon "Biblical canon") gospels share the same basic outline of the [life of Jesus](/wiki/Life_of_Jesus "Life of Jesus"): he begins his [public ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus") in conjunction with that of [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"), calls [disciples](/wiki/Disciple_(Christianity) "Disciple (Christianity)"), teaches and heals and confronts the [Pharisees](/wiki/Pharisees "Pharisees"), [dies on the cross](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), and is [raised from the dead](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus").[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThompson2006183-47) Each has its own distinctive understanding of him and his [divine](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") role[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECulpepper199966-44)[[45]](#cite_note-48) and modern scholars recognize their differences over attempts at [harmonization](/wiki/Gospel_harmony "Gospel harmony").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScholz2009192-49) The patterns of parallels and differences found in the gospels are typical of ancient biographies about actual people and history, and are thus unproblematic if not read anachronistically.[[47]](#cite_note-Keener_20192-50)

The gospels according to [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew"), [Mark](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mark "Gospel of Mark") and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") are termed the "[synoptic gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_gospels "Synoptic gospels")" (from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): σύνοψις, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ancient_Greek "Romanization of Ancient Greek"): *synopsis*, "seeing all together") because they present very similar accounts of the life of Jesus.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurkett2002217-51) Mark begins with the [baptism](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus") of the adult Jesus and the heavenly declaration that he is the [son of God](/wiki/Son_of_God "Son of God"); he gathers followers and begins his ministry, and tells his disciples that he must die in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem_in_Christianity "Jerusalem in Christianity") but that he will rise from the dead; in Jerusalem, he is at first acclaimed but then rejected, betrayed, and crucified, and when [the women](/wiki/The_Three_Marys "The Three Marys") who have followed him come to his [tomb](/wiki/Tomb_of_Jesus "Tomb of Jesus"), they [find it empty](/wiki/Empty_tomb "Empty tomb").[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoring20061–3-52) Whilst not as overt as [John's Gospel](/wiki/John%27s_Gospel "John's Gospel") (the fourth and non-synoptic gospel) scholars have found that the synoptic gospels portray Jesus as divine in various ways.[[50]](#cite_note-53) Mark apparently believes that he had a normal human parentage and birth, and makes no attempt to trace his ancestry back to King [David](/wiki/David "David") or [Adam](/wiki/Adam "Adam");[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurkett2002158-54)[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker1997125-55) it originally ended at Mark [16:8](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Mark%2016:8&version=nrsv) and had no [post-resurrection appearances](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus#Biblical_accounts "Resurrection of Jesus"), although Mark [16:7](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Mark%2016:7&version=nrsv), in which the young man discovered in the tomb instructs the women to tell "the disciples and Peter" that Jesus will see them again in Galilee, hints that the author knew of the tradition.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETelford1999148-149-56)

The authors of Matthew and Luke added different infancy and resurrection narratives to the story they found in Mark.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEve202129-57) Barker suggests that Luke supplemented Matthew's nativity by adding [Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary")'s perspective to Matthew's [Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph").[[55]](#cite_note-:5-58) Recent scholarship focuses on explaining the gospels' relationship in terms of ancient compositional practices and comparisons with other ancient historical biographers.[[56]](#cite_note-59) Each also makes subtle theological changes to Mark, though James Barker argues this is exaggerated, with ancient rhetorical practices explaining many differences in the gospels instead.[[55]](#cite_note-:5-58) The Markan miracle stories, for example, confirm Jesus' status as an emissary of God (which was Mark's understanding of the Messiah), but in Matthew they demonstrate his divinity,[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAune198759-60) and the "young man" who appears at Jesus' tomb in Mark becomes a radiant angel in Matthew.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeaton2005117,_123-61)[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1986114-62) Luke is not especially critical of the content of Mark but mainly corrects his source's grammatical syntax, lection, and style instead.[[60]](#cite_note-63)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnson2010a48-64)

John, the most overtly [theological](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology"), is the first to make [Christological](/wiki/Christological "Christological") judgements outside the context of the narrative of Jesus's life.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECulpepper199966-44) He presents a significantly different picture of Jesus's career,[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurkett2002217-51) omitting any mention of his early life, baptism, and [temptation](/wiki/Temptation_of_Christ "Temptation of Christ").[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurkett2002217-51) John knows and presupposes synoptic stories and thusly emphasizes other narratives rather than recounting every passage.[[62]](#cite_note-:14-65) The chronology is also different, describing the passage of three years in Jesus's ministry in contrast to the single year of the synoptics, placing the [cleansing of the Temple](/wiki/Cleansing_of_the_Temple "Cleansing of the Temple") at the beginning, and the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") on the day before [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover").[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAnderson201152-66) However, there are also verses such as Mark [14:49](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Mark%2014:49&version=nrsv) and Matthew [23:37](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Matthew%2023:37&version=nrsv) viewed as hints of a longer ministry in the synoptic gospels.[[64]](#cite_note-:1-67) Ancient writing practices involved such chronological displacement and changes, with even reliable biographers including [Plutarch](/wiki/Plutarch "Plutarch") displaying them.[[65]](#cite_note-68) According to Delbert Burkett, the Gospel of John is the only gospel to call Jesus God, though other scholars including [Larry Hurtado](/wiki/Larry_Hurtado "Larry Hurtado") and Michael Barber view a possible divine Christology in the synoptics.[[66]](#cite_note-web-69)[[67]](#cite_note-70)[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurkett2002216-71) In contrast to Mark, where Jesus [hides his identity as messiah](/wiki/Messianic_secret "Messianic secret"), in John he openly proclaims it.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurkett2002214-72)

### Composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gospel&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Composition")]

See also: [Authorship of the Bible](/wiki/Authorship_of_the_Bible "Authorship of the Bible") and [Oral gospel traditions](/wiki/Oral_gospel_traditions "Oral gospel traditions")

Like the rest of the New Testament, the four canonical gospels were written in [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPorter2006185-73) The Gospel of Mark probably dates from around AD 70,[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerkins1998241-18) Matthew and Luke around AD 80–90,[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReddish2011108,_144-19)[[71]](#cite_note-74) and John AD 90–100,[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELincoln200518-20)[[72]](#cite_note-:0-75) which puts their composition likely within the lifetimes of various eyewitnesses, including Jesus's own family.[[14]](#cite_note-van_Os_2011_57,_83-16)[[15]](#cite_note-Sanders_1996_5-17) Despite the traditional ascriptions, all four are anonymous, as was common for *bios* of the time; biographies by [Plutarch](/wiki/Plutarch "Plutarch") and [Suetonius](/wiki/Suetonius "Suetonius") were also originally anonymous as well.[[73]](#cite_note-76)[[74]](#cite_note-77) They are generally agreed not to represent eyewitness accounts, though this may partly be the result of dubious [form-critical](/wiki/Form_criticism "Form criticism") assumptions.[[19]](#cite_note-:4-21) Most scholars view the author of [Luke–Acts](/wiki/Luke%E2%80%93Acts "Luke–Acts") as an eyewitness to [Paul](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle").[[75]](#cite_note-78)[[76]](#cite_note-79)[[77]](#cite_note-80) However, Paul never met Jesus. Before the gospels were written, he claimed to have had a [vision of Jesus](/wiki/Conversion_of_Paul_the_Apostle "Conversion of Paul the Apostle") after his death, and later met his brother [James](/wiki/James,_brother_of_Jesus "James, brother of Jesus").[[78]](#cite_note-81)[[79]](#cite_note-82) The form-critics largely viewed the gospels as compilers of transmitted traditions, but the gospels are now seen as biographers engaging in literarily creative and imaginative form of art (which did involve claiming consulting eyewitnesses).[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReddish201113,_42-22)[[21]](#cite_note-Byrskog_2000_18–28,_69-23)[[22]](#cite_note-Rodriguez_57-24)[[23]](#cite_note-Becker_2017_39–40,_59-25)[[24]](#cite_note-:3-26) Though the texts are anonymous, many scholars have defended the attributions to Luke and John, though many others have abandoned this view.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELindarsEdwardsCourt200041-83)[[81]](#cite_note-Gathercole-84)[[82]](#cite_note-Kok_2017_13-85)[[83]](#cite_note-Brown_1997_267–82-86)

The [form critics](/wiki/Form_criticism "Form criticism") of the 20th century viewed the gospels as compilers of tradition analogous to other collections of [folktales](/wiki/Folktale "Folktale") by primitive communities steeped in [eschatology](/wiki/Eschatology "Eschatology"), but today scholars recognize the gospels as Greco-Roman biographies by conscious authors with their own theological agendas.[[84]](#cite_note-87)[[note 4]](#cite_note-91) Burkett argues the emergence of the gospels can be summarized by oral traditions passed on as unordered units, written collections of miracle stories and sayings, and [proto-gospels](/wiki/New_Testament_apocrypha "New Testament apocrypha") preceding and serving as sources for the gospels, which combined proto-gospels, written collections, and oral tradition.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurkett2002124–125-92) The [dedicatory preface of Luke](/wiki/Luke_1 "Luke 1") testifies to the existence of previous accounts of the life of Jesus.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartens2004100-93) According to Chris Keith, there is no incontrovertible evidence the gospel traditions circulated as written narratives, testimonia, or notes prior to Mark.[[90]](#cite_note-:2-94)

Mark is generally agreed to be the first gospel.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodacre200156-27) [Source criticism](/wiki/Source_criticism_(biblical_studies) "Source criticism (biblical studies)") has largely fallen out of favor in recent gospels scholarship, though some elements remain.[[24]](#cite_note-:3-26)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBoring200613–14-28) Although most scholars believe Matthew and Luke, independently used Mark and a hypothesized [Q source](/wiki/Q_source "Q source"), alternative hypotheses that posit the direct use of Matthew by Luke or vice versa without Q are increasing in popularity within scholarship.[[27]](#cite_note-:02-29)[[28]](#cite_note-TSP20222-30)[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELevine20096-31)[[note 5]](#cite_note-Markan_priority-95) Modern scholarship emphasizes the evangelists' authorial activity over positing hypothetical sources.[[91]](#cite_note-96) Honore offers a statistical classification of the number of words in the single, double, and triple traditions. [[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHonoré198695–147-97) The synoptic gospels adapt their sources more conservatively than other ancient historians, though the parallels and variations of the Synoptic gospels are typical of ancient historical biographies.[[93]](#cite_note-Kloppenborg_49–79-98)[[94]](#cite_note-Licona_2016_XIII-XIV-99) The canonical gospels represent a Jesus tradition and were enveloped by oral storytelling and performances during the early years of Christianity, rather than being redactions or literary responses to each other.[[95]](#cite_note-100) Mark, Matthew, and Luke are called the "synoptic gospels" because of their close similarities of content, arrangement, and language.[[96]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodacre20011-101) Alan Kirk praises Matthew in particular for his "scribal memory competence" and "his high esteem for and careful handling of both Mark and Q", which makes claims the latter two works are significantly theologically or historically different dubious.[[97]](#cite_note-102)[[98]](#cite_note-103) The author of John may have known the synoptics, but did not use them in the way that Matthew and Luke used Mark.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPerkins2012[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_July_2021]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (July_2021)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-104)[[100]](#cite_note-105) The increase in support for or the view that the author of the Gospel of John knew the synoptic gospels is correlated with the decline of the signs source hypothesis,[[101]](#cite_note-106) and there is currently considerable debate over the gospel's social, religious and historical context.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBynum20127,_12-38)[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurge2014309-39)[[note 6]](#cite_note-John-108)

All four also use the Jewish scriptures, by quoting or referencing passages, interpreting texts, or alluding to or echoing biblical themes.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201343–44-109) Such use can be extensive: Mark's description of the [Parousia](/wiki/Parousia "Parousia") (second coming) is made up almost entirely of quotations from scripture.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards2002403-110) Matthew is full of quotations and [allusions](/wiki/Allusion "Allusion"),[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeaton2005122-111) and although John uses scripture in a far less explicit manner, its influence is still pervasive.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELieu2005175-112) According to Wesley Allen, their source was the Greek version of the scriptures, called the [Septuagint](/wiki/Septuagint "Septuagint") and they do not seem familiar with the original Hebrew,[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen201345-113) though other scholars point out that Matthew in particular has quotations closer to the [Masoretic](/wiki/Masoretic "Masoretic") and could understand [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew "Hebrew").[[108]](#cite_note-114)[[109]](#cite_note-115)[[note 7]](#cite_note-116)

### Genre and historical reliability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gospel&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Genre and historical reliability")]

Main articles: [Historical reliability of the Gospels](/wiki/Historical_reliability_of_the_Gospels "Historical reliability of the Gospels") and [Quest for the historical Jesus](/wiki/Quest_for_the_historical_Jesus "Quest for the historical Jesus")

The scholarly consensus is that the gospels are a subset of the ancient genre of *bios*, or [biography](/wiki/Ancient_biography "Ancient biography").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELincoln2004133-4) Ancient biographies were concerned with providing examples for readers to emulate while preserving and promoting the subject's reputation and memory; the gospels were [propaganda](/wiki/Propaganda "Propaganda") and [preaching](/wiki/Kerygma "Kerygma")[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunn2005174-6) meant to convince that Jesus was a miracle-working holy man.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhrman199952-5)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVermes201332-7) As such, they present the Christian message of the second half of the first century AD,[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeithLe_Donne2012[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_July_2021]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (July_2021)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-8) and [biblical scholars](/wiki/Biblical_studies "Biblical studies") are cautious of relying on the gospels uncritically as historical documents,[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchoeps1968261–262-117)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanders2010-40)[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhrman199953-118)[[note 8]](#cite_note-a-historical-119)[[note 9]](#cite_note-nativity-120) though they provide a good idea of the career of Jesus.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanders1995b4-5-121)[[note 10]](#cite_note-Sanders_public_career-122) Bond states that many [chreia](/wiki/Chreia "Chreia") found in the gospels are literary creations rather than reservoirs of oral tradition; while many are rooted in actual history, they have been reshaped to emphasize aspects of Jesus.[[113]](#cite_note-123)

Scholars since the 19th century have viewed the gospel of John as less reliable than the Synoptic gospels.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETuckett2000523-124)[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanders199557,_70–71-125) Since the third quest, however, John's gospel is seen as having more reliability than previously thought or sometimes even more reliable than the synoptics.[[116]](#cite_note-john_JH-126)[[117]](#cite_note-127) The John, Jesus, and History Seminar has contributed to the overthrow of the previous consensus that John was of no historical value, and many scholars now see it as a source for the [Historical Jesus](/wiki/Historical_Jesus "Historical Jesus").[[118]](#cite_note-128) While there are numerous variants present in Biblical manuscripts and changes by scribes, there is a “macrolevel stability”, and scholars study the relations between the gospels with the view that the texts published in the late first and second centuries did not significantly differ from 21st century reconstructions.[[119]](#cite_note-:52-129)[[note 11]](#cite_note-131) Some of these are considered significant,[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhrman2005a69-132) an example being Matthew 1:18, altered to imply the pre-existence of Jesus.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhrman199675–76-133) For these reasons, modern scholars are cautious of relying on the gospels uncritically, and critical study can attempt to distinguish the original ideas of Jesus from those of later authors.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReddish201121–22-134)

Scholars usually agree that John is not without historical value. John provides additional support for various facts about the [Historical Jesus](/wiki/Historical_Jesus "Historical Jesus") also found in the Synoptics, such as his association with John the Baptist, choosing of twelve disciples, conflicts with religious authorities, emphasis on love, a high self-conception, and usage of aphoristic formulations.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETheissenMerz199836–37-135) [[125]](#cite_note-:15-136) John’s claims that Jesus visited Bethany and Ephraim, had a follower named Nathaniel, and that Peter was from [Bethsaida](/wiki/Bethsaida "Bethsaida") may also be historical.[[125]](#cite_note-:15-136) The narrator is presented as a witness in 1:14, and the gospel gradually identifies its narrator as the beloved disciple, notably in chapter 19.[[126]](#cite_note-:13-137)[[127]](#cite_note-:12-138) Commentators debate whether the beloved disciple is presented as the author or an authority behind the gospel.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELincoln200526-139)[[129]](#cite_note-140) Scholars tend to reject the attribution to John the Apostle, though they view the beloved disciple as the source of much of John's content.[[130]](#cite_note-Licona_2016_XIII-XIV22-141) The Synoptic Gospels are the primary sources for Christ's ministry.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanders2010-40)[[note 3]](#cite_note-Sanders_primary_sources-41)

Assessments of the reliability of the [Gospels](/wiki/Gospels "Gospels") involve not just the texts but studying the long oral and written transmission behind them using methods like memory studies and [form criticism](/wiki/Form_criticism "Form criticism"), with different scholars coming to different conclusions. There have been different views on the transmission of material that lead to the [synoptic gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_gospels "Synoptic gospels"), with various scholars arguing memory and orality reliably preserved traditions that ultimately go back to the historical Jesus.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunn1995-32)[[31]](#cite_note-Wright_1998-33)[[32]](#cite_note-Bockmuehl_2006_166–178-34)[[33]](#cite_note-McIver_2011-35) Other scholars have been more skeptical and see more changes in the traditions prior to the written Gospels.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhrman1997-36)[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEValantasisBleyleHaugh2009-37) Jeffrey Tripp observes a scholarly trend advocating for the reliability of memory and the oral gospel traditions.[[131]](#cite_note-142)

[James D.G. Dunn](/wiki/James_D.G._Dunn "James D.G. Dunn") believed that

> the earliest tradents within the Christian churches [were] preservers more than innovators [...] seeking to transmit, retell, explain, interpret, elaborate, but not create *de novo* [...] Through the main body of the Synoptic tradition [...] we have in most cases direct access to the teaching and ministry of Jesus as it was remembered from the beginning of the transmission process [...] and so fairly direct access to the ministry and teaching of Jesus through the eyes and ears of those who went about with him.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunn1995371–372-11)

Anthony Le Donne, a leading memory researcher in Jesus studies, elaborated on Dunn's thesis, basing "his historiography squarely on Dunn’s thesis that the historical Jesus is the memory of Jesus recalled by the earliest disciples."[[132]](#cite_note-Simpson-143) According to Le Donne as explained by his reviewer, Benjamin Simpson, memories are fractured, and not exact recalls of the past. Le Donne further argues that the remembrance of events is facilitated by relating it to a common story, or "type." This means the Jesus-tradition is not a theological invention of the early Church, but rather a tradition shaped and refracted through such memory "type." Le Donne too supports a conservative view on typology compared to some other scholars, transmissions involving eyewitnesses, and ultimately a stable tradition resulting in little invention in the Gospels.[[132]](#cite_note-Simpson-143) Le Donne expressed himself thusly vis-a-vis more skeptical scholars, "He (Dale Allison) does not read the gospels as fiction, but even if these early stories derive from memory, memory can be frail and often misleading. While I do not share Allison's point of departure (i.e. I am more optimistic), I am compelled by the method that came from it."[[133]](#cite_note-144)

[Dale Allison](/wiki/Dale_Allison "Dale Allison") emphasizes the weakness of human memory, referring to its 'many sins' and how it frequently misguides people. He expresses skepticism at other scholars' endeavors to identify authentic sayings of Jesus. Instead of isolating and authenticating individual pericopae, Allison advocates for a methodology focused on identifying patterns and finding what he calls 'recurrent [attestation](/wiki/Attestation "Attestation")'. Allison argues that the general impressions left by the Gospels should be trusted, though he is more skeptical on the details; if they are broadly unreliable, then our sources almost certainly cannot have preserved any of the particulars. Opposing preceding approaches where the Gospels are historically questionable and must be rigorously sifted through by competent scholars for nuggets of information, Allison argues that the Gospels are generally accurate and often 'got Jesus right'. Dale Allison finds apocalypticism to be recurrently attested, among various other themes.[[134]](#cite_note-145) Reviewing his work, Rafael Rodriguez largely agrees with Allison's methodology and conclusions while arguing that Allison's discussion on memory is too one-sided, noting that memory "is nevertheless sufficiently stable to authentically bring the past to bear on the present" and that people are beholden to memory's successes in everyday life.[[135]](#cite_note-146)

[Craig Keener](/wiki/Craig_Keener "Craig Keener"), drawing on the works of previous studies by Dunn, Alan Kirk, [Kenneth Bailey](/wiki/Kenneth_E._Bailey "Kenneth E. Bailey"), and Robert McIver, among many others, utilizes memory theory and oral tradition to argue that the Gospels are in many ways historically accurate.[[136]](#cite_note-Keener_2019-147) His work has been endorsed by [Markus Bockmuehl](/wiki/Markus_Bockmuehl "Markus Bockmuehl"), [James Charlesworth](/wiki/James_Charlesworth "James Charlesworth"), and [David Aune](/wiki/David_Aune "David Aune"), among others.[[136]](#cite_note-Keener_2019-147)
According to [Bruce Chilton](/wiki/Bruce_Chilton "Bruce Chilton") and [Craig Evans](/wiki/Craig_A._Evans "Craig A. Evans"), "...the Judaism of the period treated such traditions very carefully, and the New Testament writers in numerous passages applied to apostolic traditions the same technical terminology found elsewhere in Judaism [...] In this way they both identified their traditions as 'holy word' and showed their concern for a careful and ordered transmission of it."[[137]](#cite_note-148) [NT Wright](/wiki/NT_Wright "NT Wright") also argued for a stable oral tradition, stating "Communities that live in an oral culture tend to be story-telling communities [...] Such stories [...] acquire a fairly fixed form, down to precise phraseology [...] they retain that form, and phraseology, as long as they are told [...] The storyteller in such a culture has no license to invent or adapt at will. The less important the story, the more the entire community, in a process that is informal but very effective, will keep a close watch on the precise form and wording with which the story is told.[[138]](#cite_note-149)

Other scholars are less sanguine about oral tradition, and Valantasis, Bleyle, and Hough argue that the early traditions were fluid and subject to alteration, sometimes transmitted by those who had known Jesus personally, but more often by wandering prophets and teachers like the [Apostle Paul](/wiki/Apostle_Paul "Apostle Paul"), who did not know him personally.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEValantasisBleyleHaugh20097,_10,_14-150) Ehrman explains how the tradition developed as it was transmitted:

> You are probably familiar with the old birthday party game "[telephone](/wiki/Telephone_game "Telephone game")." A group of kids sits in a circle, the first tells a brief story to the one sitting next to her, who tells it to the next, and to the next, and so on, until it comes back full circle to the one who started it. Invariably, the story has changed so much in the process of retelling that everyone gets a good laugh. Imagine this same activity taking place, not in a solitary living room with ten kids on one afternoon, but over the expanse of the Roman Empire (some 2,500 miles across), with thousands of participants—from different backgrounds, with different concerns, and in different contexts—some of whom have to translate the stories into different languages.[[140]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhrman199744-151)

While multiple quests have been undertaken to reconstruct the historical Jesus, since the late 1990s concerns have been growing about the possibility to reconstruct a historical Jesus from the Gospel-texts.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeith2016-9) According to Dunn, "What we actually have in the earliest retellings of what is now the Synoptic tradition...are the memories of the first disciples-not Jesus himself, but the remembered Jesus. The idea that we can get back to an objective historical reality, which we can wholly separate and disentangle from the disciples' memories...is simply unrealistic."[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunn2003130-131-152)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunn2003-12)[[note 2]](#cite_note-Dunn_2003_back_to_Jesus-14) These memories can contradict and are not always historically correct, as the Gospels display. Chris Keith argues that the [Historical Jesus](/wiki/Historical_Jesus "Historical Jesus") was the one who could create these memories, both true or not. For instance, Mark and Luke disagree on how Jesus came back to the synagogue, with the likely more accurate Mark arguing he was rejected for being an artisan, while Luke portrays Jesus as literate and his refusal to heal in [Nazareth](/wiki/Nazareth "Nazareth") as cause of his dismissal. Keith does not view Luke's account as a fabrication since different eyewitnesses would have perceived and remembered differently.[[142]](#cite_note-153) According to Chris Keith, a historical Jesus is "ultimately unattainable, but can be hypothesized on the basis of the interpretations of the [early Christians](/wiki/Early_Christians "Early Christians"), and as part of a larger process of accounting for how and why early Christians came to view Jesus in the ways that they did." According to Keith, "these two models are methodologically and epistemologically incompatible," calling into question the methods and aim of the first model.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeith2016-9) Keith argues that criticism of the criteria of authenticity does not mean scholars cannot research the [Historical Jesus](/wiki/Historical_Jesus "Historical Jesus"), but rather that scholarship should seek to understand the Gospels rather than trying to sift through them for nuggets of history.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeith2012-15) Regardless of the methodological challenges [historical Jesus](/wiki/Historical_Jesus "Historical Jesus") studies have flowered in recent years; Dale Allison laments, "The publication of academic books about the historical Jesus continues apace, so much so that no one can any longer keep up; we are all overwhelmed."[[143]](#cite_note-154)

### Textual history and canonisation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gospel&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Textual history and canonisation")]

Main article: [Development of the New Testament canon](/wiki/Development_of_the_New_Testament_canon "Development of the New Testament canon")

The oldest gospel text known is [𝔓52](/wiki/Papyrus_52 "Papyrus 52"), a fragment of John dating from the first half of the second century.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFantReddish2008415-155) The creation of a Christian canon was probably a response to the career of the heretic [Marcion](/wiki/Marcion "Marcion") (c. 85–160), who established a canon of his own with just one gospel, the [Gospel of Marcion](/wiki/Gospel_of_Marcion "Gospel of Marcion"), similar to the Gospel of Luke.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhrman2005a34-156) The [Muratorian canon](/wiki/Muratorian_canon "Muratorian canon"), the earliest surviving list of books considered (by its own author at least) to form Christian scripture, included Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John. [Irenaeus of Lyons](/wiki/Irenaeus_of_Lyons "Irenaeus of Lyons") went further, stating that there must be four gospels and only four because there were [four corners of the Earth](/wiki/Four_corners_of_the_world "Four corners of the world") and thus the Church should have four pillars.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrossLivingstone2005697-1)[[146]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhrman2005a35-157) He referred to the four collectively as the "fourfold gospel" (*euangelion tetramorphon*).[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWatson201615-158)

## Non-canonical (apocryphal) gospels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gospel&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Non-canonical (apocryphal) gospels")]

Main article: [New Testament apocrypha](/wiki/New_Testament_apocrypha "New Testament apocrypha")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/El_Evangelio_de_Tom%C3%A1s-Gospel_of_Thomas-_Codex_II_Manuscritos_de_Nag_Hammadi-The_Nag_Hammadi_manuscripts.png/250px-El_Evangelio_de_Tom%C3%A1s-Gospel_of_Thomas-_Codex_II_Manuscritos_de_Nag_Hammadi-The_Nag_Hammadi_manuscripts.png)](/wiki/File:El_Evangelio_de_Tom%C3%A1s-Gospel_of_Thomas-_Codex_II_Manuscritos_de_Nag_Hammadi-The_Nag_Hammadi_manuscripts.png)

The Gospel of Thomas

The many [apocryphal](/wiki/Apocryphal_prayer "Apocryphal prayer") gospels arose from the first century onward, frequently under assumed names to enhance their credibility and authority, and often from within branches of Christianity that were eventually branded [heretical](/wiki/Heretical "Heretical").[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAune2003199–200-159) They can be broadly organised into the following categories:[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhrmanPlese2011passim-160)

* [Infancy gospels](/wiki/Infancy_gospels "Infancy gospels"): arose in the second century, including the [Gospel of James](/wiki/Gospel_of_James "Gospel of James"), also called the Protoevangelium, which was the first to introduce the concept of the [Perpetual Virginity](/wiki/Perpetual_Virginity "Perpetual Virginity") of Mary, and the [Infancy Gospel of Thomas](/wiki/Infancy_Gospel_of_Thomas "Infancy Gospel of Thomas") (not to be confused with the unrelated Coptic [Gospel of Thomas](/wiki/Gospel_of_Thomas "Gospel of Thomas")), both of which related many miraculous incidents from the life of Mary and the childhood of Jesus that are not included in the canonical gospels.
* [Ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus") gospels
* Sayings gospels and agrapha
* Passion, resurrection and post-resurrection gospels
* Gospel harmonies: in which the four canonical gospels are combined into a single narrative, either to present a consistent text or to produce a more accessible account of Jesus' life.

The apocryphal gospels can also be seen in terms of the communities which produced them:

* The [Jewish-Christian](/wiki/Jewish-Christian "Jewish-Christian") gospels are the products of Christians of Jewish origin who had not given up their Jewish identity: they regarded Jesus as the messiah of the Jewish scripture but did not agree that he was God, an idea which, although central to Christianity as it eventually developed, is contrary to Jewish beliefs.
* [Gnostic](/wiki/Gnosticism "Gnosticism") gospels uphold the idea that the universe is the product of a hierarchy of gods, of whom the [Jewish god](/wiki/God_in_Judaism "God in Judaism") is a rather low-ranking member. Gnosticism holds that Jesus was entirely "spirit", and that his earthly life and death were therefore only an appearance, not a reality. Many Gnostic texts deal not in concepts of [sin](/wiki/Sin "Sin") and [repentance](/wiki/Repentance "Repentance"), but with [illusion](/wiki/Illusion "Illusion") and [enlightenment](/wiki/Divine_illumination "Divine illumination").[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPagels1989xx-161)

## In Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gospel&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: In Islam")]

Main article: [Gospel in Islam](/wiki/Gospel_in_Islam "Gospel in Islam")

In [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), the **[Injil](/wiki/Injil "Injil")** (Arabic: الإنجيل) is considered one of the four major divine scriptures revealed by God, alongside the **[Tawrat](/wiki/Tawrat "Tawrat")** (Torah), **[Zabur](/wiki/Zabur "Zabur")** (Psalms), and the **[Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran")**.[[162]](#cite_note-173) The term *Injīl* is derived from the Syriac ܐܘܢܓܠܝܘܢ (*awongelion*), ultimately from the [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") εὐαγγέλιον (*euangelion*; *lit.* *good news* or *glad tidings*).[[163]](#cite_note-174)

According to the [Quran](/wiki/Quran "Quran"), the Injil was a divine revelation given to **[ʿĪsā (Jesus)](/wiki/Jesus_in_Islam "Jesus in Islam")**, whom Islam regards as a prophet and messenger, not the Son of God.[[164]](#cite_note-175) It is described as containing guidance and light for the Children of Israel and as confirming the scriptures that came before it.[[165]](#cite_note-176)

Mainstream Islamic belief holds that the **original Injil has been lost or altered** (*[taḥrīf](/wiki/Tahrif "Tahrif")*) over time,[[166]](#cite_note-177) and that the four canonical Gospels—[Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew"), [Mark](/wiki/Gospel_of_Mark "Gospel of Mark"), [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke"), and [John](/wiki/Gospel_of_John "Gospel of John")—do not represent this original text, although they may contain elements of Jesus’s authentic teachings.[[167]](#cite_note-178) Some classical and contemporary scholars argue that the Injil was primarily an **oral revelation** given to Jesus, similar to the Qur’an revealed to [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad"), rather than a written text authored by him.[[168]](#cite_note-179)

While the Injil holds a position of respect in Islam, it is considered **superseded by the Qur’an**,[[169]](#cite_note-180) which Muslims believe to be the final, complete, and preserved word of God. Islamic theology emphasizes that Muslims are instructed to believe in all previous scriptures in their original form, but the Qur’an is seen as the ultimate criterion (*[al-Furqān](/wiki/Al-Furqan "Al-Furqan")*) over them.[[170]](#cite_note-181)

### Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gospel&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Criticism")]

Some modern Christian scholars and apologists have criticized the Islamic view of the Injil as historically and textually problematic, arguing that there is no manuscript evidence for a distinct, lost Gospel that matches the Qur’anic description.[[171]](#cite_note-182) Others contend that the charge of *taḥrīf* ([corruption](/wiki/Corruption "Corruption")) is a later theological development rather than a contemporaneous claim from the time of Jesus.[[172]](#cite_note-183)

Muslim scholars generally respond that the Qur’an does not require the original Injil to survive in full form, only that its essential message was revealed to Jesus and has since been superseded by the Qur’an.[[173]](#cite_note-184)

"All in all, scholars identify a range of ways that the Synoptics and Acts depict Jesus as divine. Though Jesus is not overtly preexistent and "one" with God as in John, he does still arguably emerge as a divine being in the Synoptics and Acts through his "adoption," divine acts or "functions", inclusion within God's "identity," titles, intertextual connections with God, relationship to mediator gures, and his portrayal both as the object of worship and the subject of epiphanic experiences. While some of these narrative descriptors situate Jesus as a divinity in a more general sense, some situate him alongside the God of Israel, or potentially even as a manifestation of Israel's God."
